w:r.w:t: Architecture, APIs, Data Models, ML Systems

The global Applicant Tracking System (ATS) market was valued at $3.2 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $5.9 billion by 2030 at a CAGR of 9.1%. Growth is driven by AI adoption, candidate experience expectations, DEI mandates, and the expansion of hybrid/remote work models that require distributed hiring workflows. Despite market maturity, significant dissatisfaction exists: 72% of HR technology buyers report their current ATS does not meet their AI-capability expectations, and 68% of candidates abandon applications that require more than 15 minutes to complete.
Deep HRIS integration via unified data model — no data sync required between recruiting and HR. SOX, multi-country payroll linkage. Dominant in regulated industries (banking, healthcare, government).

Severely rigid UX — average implementation takes 9–18 months and costs $500K–$2M+. AI capabilities ('Illuminate') are nascent and bolt-on. Candidate experience scored 2.0/5 in independent research. Innovation cycle is 2–3 years behind pure-play ATS vendors.
Best-in-class structured hiring methodology. 400+ native integrations via REST API. Superior DEI tooling and scorecard design. Strong recruiter UX. Fastest-growing enterprise ATS 2020–2024.
No native HRIS — requires integration for every HR data point. Analytics are descriptive only (no predictive capability). Internal mobility is absent. Weak for volume/hourly hiring workflows.
Best-in-class CRM + ATS hybrid. Talent relationship management (TRM) enables proactive pipeline building. Superior nurture workflows and candidate engagement scoring. Modern React-based UX.
Acquired by Employ Inc. in 2022 — product investment has slowed. Enterprise scalability constraints above 5,000 hires/year. OFCCP and global compliance gaps. No AI beyond basic matching.
Strongest volume hiring capabilities (hourly, frontline, call center). Robust compliance module for US federal contractors (OFCCP AAP). Text Engagement module for SMS recruiting at scale.

Product is fragmented across 6+ separate modules that don't share a unified data model. UX was not redesigned since 2015. AI features are surface-level NLP. Integrations require heavy professional services.

NEXUS ATS is built on a hybrid persistence strategy: a relational PostgreSQL layer for transactional ACID-compliant data, a graph database (Amazon Neptune / Neo4j) for the skill and talent intelligence layer, a vector database (Weaviate) for semantic search and AI embeddings, and Elasticsearch for full-text search and analytics. Every core entity is described below with full field specifications.
Tenant identifier — all data partitioned by this
URL-safe identifier for career site subdomain
Logo URL, brand colors, career site config
Feature flag map for AI capabilities
Org unit for scoping requisition visibility
Channel preferences: email/Slack/in-app per event type
For session management and license optimization
Desired first day for new hire(s)
Whether salary range is shown on posting
Original JD text input by hiring manager
LLM-optimized JD with bias scan applied
Post to internal mobility board only
Flag for federal contractor AAP tracking
Candidate identifier (global, not tenant-scoped — candidates may apply to multiple orgs)
Neo4j / Neptune node ID for graph queries
Weaviate vector ID for semantic search
e.g. specific job board URL, referrer employee ID
Auto-delete trigger date per org retention policy
True if also an active employee (internal mobility)
Specific source for this application (may differ from candidate source)
Internal recruiter notes (not shown to candidate)
OFCCP disposition codes for rejected applicants
Self-ID data (gender, race, disability, veteran) — stored separately, access-controlled
Pipeline stage this interview belongs to
External calendar event ID for sync
Interview intelligence: topic coverage, STAR detection, key excerpts
Notes visible only to recruiter (not HM calibration)
System-flagged potential bias indicators in text
Whether reviewer saw candidate name/demo info
Time from open to submit (engagement signal)
Annual base in cents (avoids float precision errors)
Equity grant in shares or unit value
Annual bonus target as % of base
When offer was delivered to candidate
Market data snapshot used at offer creation
Predicted acceptance probability at send time
2.2 Skill Graph Data Model (Neo4j / Amazon Neptune)

The skill graph is the foundational intelligence layer of NEXUS ATS. Unlike relational tables, the graph models the rich relationships between entities that drive AI matching, career trajectory prediction, and team gap analysis.
id, name, aliases[], taxonomy_id, category (technical|soft|domain|language|tool|certification), market_demand_score, growth_rate_yoy, last_updated
A distinct capability unit. Linked to 50,000-node ESCO taxonomy.
Shadow node mirroring relational candidate. Properties kept minimal — rich data in Postgres.
requisition_id, title_normalized, seniority_level, industry, function, is_template
Job role node. Template roles represent archetypal positions for market benchmarking.
id, name, aliases[], industry, size_tier, hq_country, is_recognized_employer
Employer node for resume entity resolution and prestige signals.
id, name, issuer, level, expiry_years, is_verified, blockchain_hash
Degree, certification, or license. Can be linked to blockchain verification.
Open-source or portfolio project node (from GitHub enrichment).
Aggregate skill inventory for an existing team — used for gap analysis.
Core matching edge. Proficiency inferred by NLP from resume context.
Defines role skill demand profile for matching.
If candidate has Skill A, they likely have Skill B. Enables adjacent skill matching.
Skills that frequently appear together in market data — informs JD creation.
Career transition pathways. SQL → data engineering transferability.
Work history. Company prestige signals feed candidate quality model.
Computed similarity for 'candidates like your top performers' queries.
Team skill inventory for gap analysis against open roles.
Market demand signal from job posting analysis.
Query 1 — Find candidates with 80%+ skill overlap with our top performers in a role:
WITH c, COUNT(DISTINCT s) AS shared_skills
WITH c, shared_skills, COUNT(DISTINCT s2) AS total_skills
RETURN c.candidate_id, shared_skills, total_skills ORDER BY shared_skills DESC
Query 2 — Find skill gaps in a team relative to an open role:
RETURN s.name, req.importance, req.weight ORDER BY req.weight DESC
Query 3 — Discover candidates with adjacent skills for a role (no exact match required):
REST for synchronous CRUD operations (versioned at /api/v1/...)
GraphQL for flexible, nested read queries (candidate + applications + interviews in one request)
WebSockets for real-time pipeline updates and recruiter collaboration
Webhooks for outbound event notifications to integrated systems
All endpoints require OAuth 2.0 Bearer token or API key authentication
Rate limiting: 1,000 req/min per org (burst to 5,000), 100 req/min per user token
API versioning: URI versioning (/v1/, /v2/) with 24-month deprecation windows
Body: title, department_id, hiring_manager_id, locations[], comp_range, pipeline_template_id. Response: requisition object with id, req_number
Query: status, department_id, recruiter_id, location, page, per_page (max 100). Response: {data: [], meta: {total, page}}
Get single requisition with full detail
Response includes pipeline_stages, active_candidate_count, ai_insights, approval_chain_status
Partial update; triggers audit log entry for each changed field
Create or upsert candidate (deduplication by email)
Get full candidate profile with parsed resume, skill graph data, application history
Update candidate profile fields. Audit logged.
GDPR right to erasure — anonymises PII, preserves aggregate analytics
Upload and parse resume. Returns parsed_fields, skill_graph_delta, confidence_scores
Get AI match score and explanation for candidate vs requisition
Semantic candidate search. Body: {query, filters, skill_ids[], location_radius, include_internal}
Find similar candidates based on skill graph. Query: top_n (default 10), source: talent_pool|all
Generate GDPR data portability export (JSON + PDF)
Submit application. Triggers: resume parse, match score, knockout eval, acknowledgment email
Full application with candidate profile, scorecards, interview history, offer status
Advance/retract pipeline stage. Body: {stage_id, note?}. Triggers stage-change automations
Set terminal status (reject, withdraw, hire). Triggers disposition code capture if rejected
Reject with reason capture. Triggers EEOC adverse impact check. Sends candidate notification.
Export applications with full data. Supports OFCCP AAP data extract format
Score a candidate against a requisition. Body: {candidate_id, requisition_id}. Returns: {score, breakdown, top_strengths[], gaps[]}
Parse uploaded resume document. Streams results as SSE. Returns: {candidate_fields, skills[], work_history[], education[], confidence_scores}
Enhance job description: bias scan, SEO optimization, salary benchmark, SMART requirement check. Returns: {enhanced_jd, bias_flags[], seo_score, improvement_suggestions[]}
Predict hiring timeline. Body: {requisition_id}. Returns: {p50_days, p90_days, confidence, key_factors[]}
Predict offer acceptance probability. Body: {application_id, offer_params}. Returns: {probability, risk_factors[], recommendations[]}
Scan pipeline stage for adverse impact. Body: {requisition_id, stage_id}. Returns: {impact_ratio, flag_level: ok|warn|alert, affected_groups[], recommendations[]}
Analyze scorecard set for calibration issues. Body: {application_id}. Returns: {rater_variance, halo_effect_flags[], consensus_score, calibration_recommendations[]}
Find matching past candidates for new role. Body: {requisition_id, max_results, min_match_score}. Returns: ranked candidate list with match explanations
Candidate chatbot message. Body: {session_id, message, application_id}. Returns: {reply, action?, next_prompt?}
Recruiter copilot message. Body: {session_id, message, context}. Returns: {reply, actions[], data_payload?}
application_id, candidate_id, requisition_id, source_channel, applied_at, match_score
application_id, candidate_id, requisition_id, offer_id, start_date, hired_at
HRIS Integration (Workday, SAP SuccessFactors, Oracle HCM)
Protocol: REST API or SFTP file exchange with AES-256 encrypted payload
Sync frequency: Real-time webhooks for hire events; nightly batch for org structure, cost centers, job families
Data pushed to HRIS on hire: candidate name, email, start date, title, department, cost center, comp details, manager, location, employment type
Data pulled from HRIS: org hierarchy, active employees (for internal mobility), job families, comp grades, open headcount budgets
Authentication: OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow with rotating secrets (90-day rotation)
Recruiter System Connect (RSC) API for job posting and candidate import
LinkedIn Apply integration: candidates apply with LinkedIn profile, pre-populating ATS fields
InMail send via API for direct outreach from ATS recruiter view
Job posting sync: NEXUS → LinkedIn via Job Posting API with structured data (salary, skills, benefits)
Talent Insights API: market compensation benchmarks, skill supply/demand data
OAuth 2.0 per-user token for Google Calendar API and Microsoft Graph API
Read availability: fetch free/busy slots with 15-minute granularity
Create events: NEXUS creates calendar events with video link, interview guide attachment, scorecard deep-link
Sync updates: calendar changes sync back to NEXUS interview record via push notifications (Google) and subscriptions (M365)
Room booking: integrate with Google Room Resource or Exchange room management for onsite interviews
Trigger: NEXUS sends hire data to background check vendor API on offer acceptance (with candidate consent captured in ATS)
Webhook receipt: background check status updates (clear/consider/dispute) push back to NEXUS offer record
Adverse action: NEXUS triggers adverse action workflow with pre-adverse and adverse action letters (FCRA compliant)
Data minimization: only required fields transmitted (name, DOB, SSN last 4, addresses) — no unnecessary PII sharing

NEXUS ATS operates five distinct ML systems, each independently trained, evaluated, and deployed. All models run on AWS SageMaker with real-time inference endpoints. Models are versioned, A/B tested, and monitored for drift. A central ML Platform team owns the training pipelines; individual product teams consume model outputs via the AI API layer.
Stage 1 — Document Ingestion: PDF/DOCX/RTF → text extraction (PyMuPDF for PDF, python-docx). Image-based PDFs → OCR (AWS Textract with layout analysis).
Stage 2 — Language Detection: langdetect library → route to language-specific parsing model.

Stage 3 — Section Segmentation: fine-tuned BERT classifier trained on 500K labelled resume sections. Labels: CONTACT, SUMMARY, EXPERIENCE, EDUCATION, SKILLS, CERTIFICATIONS, PROJECTS, AWARDS, LANGUAGES, OTHER. F1 > 0.97.

Stage 4 — Entity Extraction: custom NER model (SpaCy + fine-tuned RoBERTa) extracts: person name, email, phone, URLs, company names, job titles, dates, degree types, institutions, skills, certifications.

Stage 5 — Skill Normalization: extracted skill strings → embedding similarity search against 50K-node skill taxonomy. Returns canonical skill ID + confidence score. Threshold: accept if cosine similarity > 0.82.

Stage 6 — Work History Structuring: LLM prompt (GPT-4o or Claude 3 Haiku for cost efficiency) to resolve ambiguous entries, infer missing end dates, classify seniority level from title and responsibilities.
Stage 7 — Graph Update: write parsed data to Neo4j (skill nodes + HAS_SKILL edges) and Weaviate (update candidate embedding vector).
Stage 8 — Confidence Scoring: per-field confidence scores attached to output. Fields below 0.70 confidence flagged for recruiter review.
Monthly, or when F1 drops below 0.94 on production sample
Quarterly, or when new skill taxonomy version released
Skill taxonomy + 5M job posting skill mentions
XGBoost on structured features (title, years_exp, reports_to, team_size)
The match score is a weighted composite of five independent sub-scores, each computed by a separate model. Weights are configurable per org and role type. Default weights shown below:
Graph similarity (Jaccard + semantic) between candidate HAS_SKILL edges and role REQUIRES_SKILL edges. Adjacent skills contribute at 60% value.
Resume skills, skill graph HAS_SKILL edges, implied skills via IMPLIES_SKILL
Embedding cosine similarity between candidate work history embedding and role description embedding. Role title normalization applied.
Work history text, company prestige signal, title-to-level mapping
Graph path analysis: does candidate's career progression pattern match that of high-performers in this role? Compares against SIMILAR_TO edges.
Work history sequence, seniority progression, industry transitions
Rule-based: remote role = 100%; onsite = proximity score based on candidate location vs role location; relocation flag boosts score.
Candidate location, role location, candidate relocation preference
Rule-based + ML: degree level match, field relevance (embedding similarity), institution rank if applicable. Penalizes credential inflation (PhD required for entry role).
Education entities from resume, role education requirement field
Recency and quality of candidate engagement with the org: career site visits, email opens, referral status, past application quality.
Candidate activity logs, communication engagement events
Every match score is accompanied by a structured explanation object delivered to the recruiter UI:
"top_strengths": ["Strong Spark + dbt stack match","FAANG experience signal"],
seniority_level, department, location_type, is_niche_skill, headcount_target, comp_competitiveness_index
External data from LinkedIn Talent Insights API + proprietary job board scraper
Org-specific calibration improves accuracy by 30%+ vs generic model
Hiring seasonality is significant — Q1 and Q4 move 20% faster than Q2/Q3 on average

Model: Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost) with quantile regression for confidence intervals. Output: median days estimate (p50), p90 upper bound, and key factor explanations. Training data: 5 years of historical hiring data across customer orgs (anonymised, federated). RMSE target: < 7 days on 30-day horizon.
Model: Logistic Regression ensemble + SHAP for feature attribution. Output: 0-100 probability with top 3 risk factors. Calibrated against actual accept/decline outcomes. AUC target: > 0.82.

Layer 1 — JD Scanner: NLP classifier trained on 50K human-labelled JD sentences. Labels: gendered_language, age_bias, credential_inflation, exclusionary_requirement, cultural_fit_ambiguity. Model: fine-tuned DistilBERT. Precision/Recall targets: > 0.90.

Layer 2 — Funnel Adverse Impact: daily computation of 4/5ths rule across protected characteristics (race, gender, age_group, disability_status, veteran_status) at each pipeline stage. Threshold alerts at 0.80 ratio (warn) and 0.65 (critical).

Layer 3 — Scorecard Language Analysis: NLP scan of free-text scorecard fields for coded language patterns (e.g., 'articulate' as a racial proxy, 'culture fit' without definition). Alerts reviewer before submission.
Layer 4 — Rater Calibration: statistical analysis of interviewer score distributions. Flags outlier raters (systematic leniency, severity, or halo effect). Weekly report to TA leader.

Layer 5 — Model Fairness Monitoring: continuous demographic parity and equalized odds measurement on AI match scores. Alert when disparity exceeds 0.10 between any two protected groups. Triggers model audit.

NEXUS ATS explicitly does NOT use facial expression analysis, voice tone affect scoring, or physical appearance features in any AI model. These inputs carry documented discriminatory risk and legal exposure under the Illinois Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act and equivalent laws.
Auth validation, rate limiting, routing, request logging, WAF integration
CRUD for requisitions, approval workflow engine, job board publishing orchestration
Candidate CRUD, deduplication, profile enrichment, GDPR data management
Application lifecycle, stage management, disposition tracking, EEOC data handling
Scheduling, calendar sync, video link generation, reminder orchestration
Email/SMS/WhatsApp send, template rendering, delivery tracking, unsubscribe management
Real-time in-app notifications, WebSocket broadcast, push notifications (mobile)
Offer generation, e-signature orchestration, approval routing, acceptance/decline capture
Routes AI requests to appropriate models; handles async jobs, result caching, fallback
Document ingestion, NLP pipeline execution, skill graph updates
Candidate-role match scoring, batch ranking, talent rediscovery
Report generation, dashboard data APIs, predictive model serving for analytics
EEOC/OFCCP report generation, GDPR consent management, data retention enforcement, audit logs
CRM functionality, nurture campaign management, talent community portal backend
Full-text candidate/requisition search, faceted filters, search analytics
HRIS connectors, job board publisher, background check triggers, webhook delivery
SSO/OIDC/SAML, JWT issuance, MFA, session management, API key management
All inter-service communication for non-synchronous operations flows through Kafka. This decouples services, enables event replay for debugging, and supports the audit log requirement.
AI Orchestration (match scoring), Compliance (adverse impact check), Analytics, Notification, Communication
Resume Parser, Matching Engine, Talent Pool, Search (index update), Analytics
Communication (reminders), Notification, AI Orchestration (scorecard prompts), Analytics
Communication (offer delivery), Integration Hub (HRIS notify), Analytics, Compliance
Application Service (update match score), Analytics, Compliance (bias alerts)
Integration Hub (delivery to customer endpoints with retry)
Every service is deployed as a Kubernetes Deployment with the following base configuration, scaled by Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) based on CPU and custom metrics:
All US customer data, global control plane
Auto-failover to us-west-2 if region fails (RTO < 15min)
Promotes to primary automatically on health check failure
All EU customer data (GDPR data residency)
Lawful basis for processing candidate data: Legitimate interest (initial application) + Explicit consent (talent pool retention beyond application cycle)

Consent capture: Granular checkboxes at application submission. Separate consents for: (1) process this application, (2) retain data for future roles, (3) share with recruitment partners. Each timestamped, IP-logged, and immutably stored.
Consent withdrawal: One-click opt-out in every email footer. Candidate portal self-service. API endpoint for system-triggered withdrawal. Processing stops within 24 hours.
Data minimization: Collect only fields required for the role. Optional fields clearly labelled. EEO data collected on separate, access-controlled form with clear purpose explanation.
Candidate portal: 'Download my data' generates JSON + PDF export of all stored data, processing history, and third parties data was shared with
Erasure request triggers: anonymization of all PII fields (name→'Deleted', email→hash, phone→null), deletion of resume document, graph node anonymization. Aggregate analytics retained.
Candidate can edit all profile fields in self-service portal. Changes audit-logged.
Structured JSON export + standardized CSV of all candidate data. Machine-readable format per W3C Data Portability standards.
Candidate can flag account as 'restricted' — data retained but not used in any AI processing or new matching
Right to object to automated decisions
AI recommendations always have human-in-the-loop gate. Candidate can request human review of any AI-assisted decision. Flag triggers recruiter notification.

NEXUS captures 'internet applicants' per the four-part OFCCP test: (1) submitted expression of interest via internet, (2) qualified per basic requirements, (3) not withdrawn, (4) employer considered them for a position.

Disposition codes: full OFCCP disposition code library pre-built (Not Considered, Not Most Qualified, Position Filled Before Review, etc.). Codes required for every rejected applicant on OFCCP-tracked requisitions.
Record retention: All application records for OFCCP-tracked positions retained for 2 years from closure (5 years for executive roles). Automated retention policies enforce this.
The adverse impact engine runs on configurable schedules (daily for high-volume roles, weekly for standard roles) and computes the 4/5ths (80%) rule across all EEOC-protected characteristics:
Adverse Impact Ratio = (Selection rate for protected group) / (Selection rate for highest-selected group)
Alert thresholds: ratio < 0.80 → WARNING (yellow); ratio < 0.65 → CRITICAL (red); ratio < 0.50 → ESCALATE to legal
Analysis computed at: initial application screen (knockout), resume review, phone screen, onsite interview, offer extended, offer accepted.
Standard deviation test: Fisher's Exact Test applied when sample size < 30. Chi-square test for larger samples.
Report outputs: EEOC-1 equivalent data, flow statistics, impact ratio by stage, trend over time, peer benchmark comparison.
6.3 EU AI Act Compliance (High-Risk AI System)
Hiring AI systems are classified as 'high-risk' under Annex III of the EU AI Act (effective August 2026 for new systems). NEXUS ATS compliance approach:
Documented risk assessment for each AI model. Quarterly review. Risk register maintained by AI governance team.
Training data documentation: source, processing steps, demographic distribution, known limitations. Data quality metrics published in model cards.
Model cards for each AI system: architecture, training data summary, performance metrics, known limitations, intended use, prohibited use.
Audit log of every AI recommendation, input data hash, model version, output score, human decision made. Retained 5 years.
Recruiters informed when AI is involved in any recommendation. Score explanations required. AI involvement disclosed in candidate-facing communications.
AI cannot independently reject or hire any candidate. Every AI recommendation requires human confirmation. Override capability always present.
Continuous production monitoring: drift detection, demographic parity measurement, accuracy on held-out labeled samples monthly.
Third-party audit of high-risk AI systems before deployment. Annual re-audit. Certification documentation maintained.
TOTP (authenticator app) or hardware key required for all privileged roles. Enforced at IdP level.
Privileged actions (data export, config change) require just-in-time access approval via PagerDuty or Teleport.
AES-256-GCM via AWS KMS. Customer-managed keys (BYOK) for enterprise tier. Key rotation every 90 days.
TLS 1.2 and below disabled at load balancer. HSTS with 1-year max-age. Certificate pinning on mobile apps.
Istio service mesh with mTLS between all services. NetworkPolicy denies all traffic by default; allow-listed only.
Trivy scans every image in CI/CD. Critical/high CVEs block deployment. Weekly rescan of running containers.
Annual third-party pentest. Quarterly internal DAST with OWASP ZAP. Bug bounty program on HackerOne.
AWS CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Datadog SIEM. Alert rules for: impossible travel, bulk data export, privilege escalation, unusual API patterns.
All API responses run through PII detector. Unnecessary PII masked based on requester role. Logs scrubbed before leaving secure boundary.
PostgreSQL: continuous WAL streaming to S3. Point-in-time recovery to any second within 30 days. Daily snapshot tests. Annual DR exercise.

The autonomous hiring agent is an LLM-orchestrated workflow system (built on LangGraph or similar agentic framework) designed for high-volume, well-defined roles (customer service, warehouse, retail, call center). The agent manages the hiring funnel from application receipt to conditional offer issuance, with humans reviewing exceptions and all final offers.
Parse resume, run knockout screen, compute match score
Auto-advance if match_score >= 65 AND knockout_passed == true
Adverse impact check on every advance decision. Rate: monitor 4/5ths rule in real time.
Send AI-drafted SMS/email with 3–5 screening questions. Parse and evaluate responses using LLM.
Auto-advance if response quality score >= 0.70 AND no disqualifying answer
LLM response evaluation prompt includes bias guardrails. Human spot-checks 5% of evaluations.
Send Aria (candidate chatbot) link to async video platform (HireVue/Spark Hire integration). Transcribe and score.
Auto-advance if competency signal score >= 0.68 (configurable threshold)
No — unless score is 0.55–0.68 (human review band)
No emotion/biometric scoring. Text and content analysis only. STAR detection only.
Aria offers 3 available slots from interviewer calendar. Confirms booking.
Automated scheduling with no human input
Candidate can always request to speak with a human recruiter.
Agent prepares offer document using pre-approved comp band and benefit details for role
Offer prepared automatically within approved comp range
YES — hiring manager approves before sending
Every offer requires human approval. Agent cannot send autonomously.
Trigger background check vendor API on offer acceptance. Monitor for result.
Clear → auto-notify recruiter. Consider/Adverse → escalate to HR
YES — adverse action requires human review
FCRA pre-adverse and adverse action letter generated but sent only after human confirmation.
At any step: candidate requests human, score is in ambiguous band, system confidence is low, or bias alert fires
Route immediately to human recruiter queue with full context
Candidate can escalate to human at any point via Aria chatbot.
Blockchain: Hyperledger Fabric (permissioned) or Polygon PoS (public, lower cost). Permissioned preferred for enterprise privacy requirements.
Standard: W3C Verifiable Credentials (VC) 2.0 + W3C DID (Decentralized Identifiers)
Issuer network: universities, professional associations, certification bodies. Each issuer runs a node and signs credentials with their DID-anchored private key.
Candidate wallet: NEXUS provides a lightweight credential wallet (mobile app + web). Candidates receive VCs from issuers and selectively disclose to employers.
Verification flow: Candidate presents VC to NEXUS ATS → ATS resolves issuer DID from blockchain → verifies credential signature → returns verified status in < 500ms.
employee_id, name, email, department, title, manager_id, location, hire_date, employment_type, skills (if available), performance_tier (if accessible)
performance_rating, peer_review_summary, goal_completion_pct (aggregated — not individual review text)
desired_roles[], preferred_locations[], open_to_relocation, career_goal_summary, skills_developing[], not_interested_in[]
Employee job board: Separate internal-facing career site with all open roles. Employees can apply with pre-filled profile. Applications flagged as internal for differentiated process.
Internal-first window: Configurable 3–7 day window where roles are posted internally before external publishing. Automatic extension if qualified internal candidates apply.
Skills-to-role pathway: 'Roles you could grow into' section on employee portal. Shows open roles within 1–2 skill hops from current profile. Links directly to learning content to close gaps.
Manager transparency controls: Employee chooses whether their manager can see they're exploring internal roles (three modes: fully private, notify on application, always visible).
Succession planning integration: TA leaders can designate 'succession-tracked' roles. Internal candidates for these roles get additional consideration flags visible to senior leadership.
Best instruction-following for nuanced language analysis. Constitutional AI alignment reduces discriminatory output risk.
Strongest structured data extraction. JSON mode output reliability.
Low latency, cost-effective for high-volume conversational use. Fast enough for real-time chat.
Complex reasoning, long context window for pipeline analysis, NL to SQL translation.
Long context window (128K), strong extraction of structured insights from unstructured text.
Template-filling task; cost and speed matter. Quality sufficient for pre-reviewed template.
Latency-critical (runs on every scorecard save). Cost-effective at scale. Privacy: no PII leaves org boundary.
Cost-efficient for batch workloads. No per-token cost. Acceptable quality for bulk pre-scoring.

All PII (candidate name, email, phone, SSN, DOB) is redacted from prompts before sending to any third-party LLM API. Candidate ID tokens replace identifiers in all LLM prompts. PII is reattached after response is received. Zero PII leaves the NEXUS secure boundary to external model providers.
Kubernetes, CI/CD, monitoring, incident response, security patching, DR runbooks
Staff engineer (lead), 4× senior engineers, 2× mid engineers
Requisition, application, interview, offer, compliance microservices
Staff ML engineer (lead), 3× senior ML engineers, 1× ML ops engineer
Resume parsing, matching engine, predictive models, bias detection, LLM integration
Staff data engineer (lead), 2× data engineers, 1× analytics engineer (dbt)
Data warehouse, dbt models, Snowflake, Kafka pipelines, reporting APIs
Staff frontend engineer (lead), 4× senior engineers, 2× mid engineers
React SPA, recruiter UX, candidate portal, admin UI, design system
iOS and Android candidate app, push notifications, mobile interview UX
HRIS connectors, job board publisher, webhook delivery, background check APIs
Threat modeling, security review, pentest coordination, compliance audit support
Roadmap, requirements, user research, design system, accessibility
Test automation, performance testing, regression, contract testing (Pact)
Total team at full scale: approximately 38–40 people (including 4 non-engineering product/design). MVP team (Phase 0–1): 12–15 engineers recommended.
Per EC2 instance (no EKS node charge)
SES for bulk, SendGrid for transactional

The MVP delivers a working, deployable ATS with the minimal viable feature set to begin customer pilots. It is built as a modular monolith (a single deployable unit with clear internal service boundaries), enabling rapid development while maintaining the ability to extract microservices as load demands it.
EKS cluster provisioned, CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions → ArgoCD), PostgreSQL on RDS, Redis, S3, monitoring (Datadog), staging + prod environments
Multi-tenant data model, Auth service (SSO + email/password + MFA), RBAC middleware, API gateway, audit logging foundation
Requisition CRUD + approval workflow, Pipeline stage management, Application submission (candidate-facing form), Basic candidate profile
Resume upload → text extraction → basic NER (SpaCy pre-trained model, no custom training yet) → skill tagging. Good enough for pilot, refined in Phase 1.
Google/O365 calendar integration, Interview creation, basic self-scheduling link, Video link generation (Zoom SDK)
Offer letter template, DocuSign integration, offer send/accept/decline workflow
Basic analytics dashboard, 5 HRIS integrations (Workday, BambooHR, Rippling, HiBob, Personio), LinkedIn job posting, Indeed posting, candidate portal, mobile-responsive
Code coverage: minimum 80% unit test coverage, 60% integration test coverage on all new service code
API contract testing: Pact consumer-driven contract tests between every service pair. No contract breakage allowed in CI.
Performance: every new API endpoint must pass load test at 10x expected production traffic before merge to main
Security: no Critical or High CVEs in container images. SAST scan (Semgrep) must pass. No secrets in code (Trufflehog).
Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for all candidate-facing UI. Automated axe-core scan in CI + manual quarterly audit.
Bias: any new AI model must pass fairness testing (demographic parity < 0.10 disparity) before production deployment
Documentation: OpenAPI spec updated for any API change. Runbook updated for any new infrastructure component. Decision log entry for any architectural decision.
This document is the single source of truth for the NEXUS ATS product architecture.
